county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Muskogee County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Muskogee, which was the seat of the Union Agency (BIA) for all Five Civilized Tribes. Central hub for Indian Territory records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Muskogee County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. Muskogee was the seat of the Union Agency (Bureau of Indian Affairs) overseeing all Five Civilized Tribes, making it the most important center for Indian Territory records. The Five Civilized Tribes Museum in Muskogee has tribal historical materials. NARA Fort Worth holds Union Agency records, Dawes Commission records, allotment files, and tribal court records. Creek Nation enrollment records and Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen) are critical. The US Court for the Western District of Indian Territory sat in Muskogee. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds extensive Indian Territory court records and newspapers. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Muskogee had a significant African American community -- freedmen records and All-Black community records are important. Bacone College (founded 1880 for Native Americans) has historical records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Muskogee County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Cherokee Outlet run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Noble County formed 1893 from the Cherokee Outlet (land run). County seat is Perry. Named for John W. Noble, Secretary of the Interior. Adjacent to Ponca and Otoe-Missouria tribal areas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Noble County was formed from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. Perry, the county seat, was a boom town during the run. County Clerk records begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims. The Cherokee Strip Museum in Perry has pioneer family records and photographs. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. Noble County is also adjacent to the Otoe-Missouria and Ponca tribal areas -- check tribal enrollment records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. The Noble County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Noble County; County Clerk deed books from 1893

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Nowata County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Nowata. The name is a Cherokee word. Oil-producing county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Nowata County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. Cherokee Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls, and allotment records are critical for pre-statehood research. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency records. County Clerk records in Nowata begin at statehood (1907). The county has mixed Cherokee and Delaware (Lenape) tribal heritage. Oil production in the county generated lease and royalty records. The Nowata County Historical Society Museum has local materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Nowata County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1907 (shortly after statehood) destroyed initial county records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Okfuskee County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Okemah. Courthouse fire 1907 destroyed initial records. Home to Boley, one of the largest All-Black towns in the US.
county_metadata.research_tips: Okfuskee County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. A courthouse fire shortly after statehood in 1907 destroyed initial county records. Creek Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records at NARA Fort Worth are essential -- both for tribal research and as alternatives to destroyed county records. The county had several All-Black towns including Boley (established 1903), one of the largest and most famous All-Black towns in the US. African American research should include freedmen records, All-Black town community records, and Boley city records. County Clerk records in Okemah are incomplete for the earliest period. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Okfuskee County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (1889 Land Run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Oklahoma County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from the 1889 Land Run. County seat is Oklahoma City, the state capital (since 1910). Most populous Oklahoma county. Home to the Oklahoma Historical Society headquarters.
county_metadata.research_tips: Oklahoma County is the most populous county and home to Oklahoma City, the state capital. Formed after the 1889 Land Run, it has among the oldest and most complete records in the state. County Clerk records begin from 1889. BLM GLO land patents document original Land Run claims. The Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS) headquarters in Oklahoma City is the central state repository, holding statewide collections including Indian Territory records, territorial government records, newspapers, manuscripts, maps, and photographs. The Oklahoma City Metropolitan Library System has an exceptional genealogy department with city directories, newspaper indexes, and family files. For African American research, records of the 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre aftermath (some displaced families moved to Oklahoma City), freedmen communities, and All-Black communities are important. Oil boom records (1920s-1930s Oklahoma City field) include lease records and corporation commission files. FamilySearch has extensive Oklahoma collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Oklahoma County Clerk online index for recent land records; historical deed books at the county office; BLM GLO patents for 1889 Land Run claims

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Okmulgee County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Okmulgee, former capital of the Creek Nation. Creek Council House Museum.
county_metadata.research_tips: Okmulgee County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. Okmulgee was the capital of the Creek Nation, and the Creek Council House (now a museum) served as the tribal government center. Creek Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records are critical. NARA Fort Worth holds Creek Agency and Union Agency records. The county had All-Black towns and significant freedmen communities. Oil production in the county generated lease and royalty records, particularly guardianship records for minor allottees. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Creek Council House Museum has tribal historical materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Okmulgee County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Osage County formed 1907 at statehood from the Osage Nation reservation. County seat is Pawhuska. Largest Oklahoma county by area. Coterminous with the Osage reservation. Unique Osage headright (mineral rights) system. 'Reign of Terror' (1920s oil wealth murders).
county_metadata.research_tips: Osage County is the largest county in Oklahoma by area and is coterminous with the Osage Nation reservation. The Osage maintained mineral rights (headrights) under their 1906 Allotment Act, making Osage headright and royalty records uniquely important genealogical sources. The 'Reign of Terror' (1920s murders of Osage for their oil headrights, documented in 'Killers of the Flower Moon') generated FBI investigation records and court records. Osage Nation tribal records (Osage Allotment Roll 1906, annuity rolls, headright records) are at the Osage Nation headquarters in Pawhuska and NARA Fort Worth. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Osage County Historical Society Museum in Pawhuska has local materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds extensive Osage-related records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. Osage agency records at NARA are among the most detailed Indian agency records in existence.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Osage County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Cherokee Nation / Quapaw Agency, Indian Territory
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Ottawa County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation and Quapaw Agency. County seat is Miami. Home to nine federally recognized tribes. Lead/zinc mining heritage (Picher mining district).
county_metadata.research_tips: Ottawa County was part of the Cherokee Nation and Quapaw Agency in Indian Territory. Multiple smaller tribal nations have communities here: Quapaw, Peoria, Miami, Modoc, Ottawa, Wyandotte, Eastern Shawnee, and Seneca-Cayuga. Tribal enrollment records at respective tribal headquarters and NARA Fort Worth are essential. The Quapaw Agency records at NARA are particularly important. County Clerk records in Miami begin at statehood (1907). The Dobson Museum in Miami has local history materials. Lead and zinc mining in the Tar Creek area (Picher mining district) brought immigrant workers and generated mine employment records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Ottawa County Genealogical Society has local materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Ottawa County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the Ottawa County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Pawnee lands opening)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pawnee County formed 1893 when Pawnee tribal lands were opened. County seat is Pawnee, headquarters of the Pawnee Nation. Named for the Pawnee people. Home of Pawnee Bill (Gordon W. Lillie).
county_metadata.research_tips: Pawnee County was organized in 1893 when Pawnee tribal lands were opened to non-Indian settlement. The Pawnee Nation (removed from Nebraska in 1874-1875) maintains its headquarters and tribal enrollment office in Pawnee. Pawnee tribal records (annuity rolls, allotment records, agency records) are at NARA Fort Worth and the Pawnee Nation. County Clerk records begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document non-Indian settler claims. The Pawnee Bill Ranch and Museum has local history materials (Gordon W. 'Pawnee Bill' Lillie was a showman). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. The Pawnee County Historical Society has cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Pawnee County; County Clerk deed books from 1893

source_profiles.source_name: Pawnee County Deed Books / BLM GLO Patents -- County Clerk
source_profiles.repository: Pawnee County Clerk, Pawnee / Bureau of Land Management
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Pawnee County from 1893; BLM GLO federal land patents document original claims from the territorial opening; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1893; GLO patents are complete for federal land entries

source_profiles.source_name: Pawnee County Probate Records -- District Court
source_profiles.repository: Pawnee County District Court, Pawnee
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Pawnee County; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; guardianship records for minor allottees are particularly important in former Indian Territory counties

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (1889 Land Run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Payne County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from the 1889 Land Run. County seat is Stillwater, home to Oklahoma State University. Named for David L. Payne, leader of the 'Boomer' movement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Payne County was one of the original Oklahoma Territory counties formed after the 1889 Land Run. Stillwater, the county seat, is home to Oklahoma State University. County Clerk records begin from 1889, among the oldest in Oklahoma. BLM GLO land patents document original Land Run claims. The Sheerar Museum and Oklahoma State University Library Special Collections have local and regional history materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. The Payne County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions and family histories. David L. Payne and the 'Boomer' movement (illegal settlement attempts 1880-1884) preceded the legal opening. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Payne County; County Clerk deed books from 1890
